speaker: interviewee
text: Director? And I OK? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He doesn't know yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: May 25, 1995. Interview with Lola Kay. This is tape number four.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-mm.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Let's get into the story about the outside commander.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I asked [NON-ENGLISH], OK, I was so full of that smoke from the crematorium. You haven't seen any sun. It was so gray, dark, no sky. And I wanted to go out, out of that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she figured, first, I'm too young. I'm not strong enough, you know, for the [NON-ENGLISH]. I take that chance. And I wanted to go out. 20 miles you walk. She-- she said, OK. I-- I just like the fresh air, 20 miles away were a different. I was digging, you know, the-- for the farmers to plant things. So you walked in swamps. OK? You were digging to dry up the-- for them to plant things. So you became a member of another work group that went outside the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: I ask, yea. Outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was in-- in there, but they were [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: I know, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were inside. In the camp, they worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was not too much to do like-- like repair and torn clothes or things like that. And when you sit, you feel-- you get sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So you got out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Out.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's-- that's what I liked even walking. It did me, you know, much better. So I finally had two sisters. They were also, you know, the Rabinowitzs. They had [NON-ENGLISH] all over that we were [NON-ENGLISH] with them from Lodz. The two sisters were working on they [NON-ENGLISH] camera. They-- when the transport came in from Europe all over, wherever they brought people,

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They left everything. And this-- they segregated things, which were shipped to Germany for the soldiers fur or-- or-- or things to wear. They were interested, because the war wasn't going too good for them in Russia. So they needed warm clothes, sweaters mainly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: She brought me a sweater, like a lemon. It was the most beautiful yellow turtleneck, like a lemon yellow sweater. And I put this sweater on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went on that [NON-ENGLISH]. Somebody got and eye on me. And they came into the kapo. She took care. She was one of the convicts taking care. She had like a man's voice with a whip, you know, next to her. One move you made, if you-- if you straightened out, you always have to dig and dig. If you straighten out, you-- you got it over your head or what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she came in. And she said, you're going. She took the shovel. I should put the shovel down. And I said, where am I going? You'll see. She took me to a place. It was like a-- a-- warehouse, something you know, they keep tools or what. A shed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shed. And there was a table there. And I walked in there. I didn't know what's going to happen to me. So then I saw a middle-aged fat man walks in. He was one of the prisoners, too, but a German. He was a convict. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he walked in. And I didn't know. I thought he was going to kill me or what. Then he said to take my clothes off. There was a table there, and lay down on the table. I just started moving backwards. And I-- to the-- to a wall, and I propped myself up, and I looked. And I just make believe I don't know what he's-- to whom he's talking. And he started smoke-- he smoked. And he just crushed one cigarette after the other and, you know, and lit up another one. And I didn't move. I didn't made a move. I just stood there while he looked and looked at me. He just throw down one cigarette after the other. And he just cursed, something he said in German. And he walked out. After he walked out, I walked out. And I joined the, you know, the others. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So that's what she did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she like sold you to him for his--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Enjoyment. Then--

speaker: interviewee
text: She got a pack of cigarettes probably for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you did nothing, so she was in trouble now.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in trouble. [LAUGHTER] And she split my-- she split my face. She clubbed me in my eye. And the blood was start gushing. I was all over bloody. And I came in into the camp. And she was in trouble there, because the-- there-- there's no restriction for them to hit, but not like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK? I mean, to bruise you or hit you, but not like that. She smashed me, and I was like a triangle open. The-- the skin was open.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I blew up. The whole face was, you know, swell up. And they took me to-- supposed to be [INAUDIBLE] called a make believe. They had the nurses there, and I patched up. The swelling was so that you didn't see if the eye is in or not. And I was on the block, you know, until the swelling went down finally and saw the eye is still in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that affect the vision in that eye.

speaker: interviewee
text: Affect the vision. Yep. So the retina, I'm going ever since, you know, to a retinal specialist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, did it detach the retina? Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it didn't detach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but almost.  It became weak.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's weak, and it's no good. So that's-- that was one thing. Then I stopped-- the [NON-ENGLISH] stopped me from going out the-- outside commanders.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, you know, that was slow, very slow healing. Then my-- my feet started, you know, to hurt. I started to-- lack of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Circulation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nutrition. And so I get vitamin deficiency, so I started to have open wounds. And at-- you know, at pus came-- come out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Became infected.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I have marks all over. And so she used to push me, [INAUDIBLE], she pushed me. She used to hide me in-- inside the hood.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: For the roll call in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Inspections, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when the-- when you had any lesion or anything on your body, you know, you're gone on the 25th block.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this woman virtually saved your life for a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: She saved. Yeah. She was hiding me. And she pushed me in the back of the-- you know, when you were standing under the roll call. So she pushed me not in the front, in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they wouldn't see anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: They wouldn't see it. So then came-- if I'm going to tell you the whole thing, what I went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened when the Czechoslovakian prisoners came.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Czechoslovakian prisoners?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you said that they-- they started separating the prisoners. And you were there. And they pushed you, and you were in the wrong line.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. That was-- that was the big, big, big selection.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, what happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they had to make room for all those people coming in with the transports.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Yeah. You told me that they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They are  expected.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were coming in with the transports, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So they had to make room. So there was one of the biggest selection that Mengele had then, and I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were part of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: --there. And I was, you know, there. And it was then everybody thought that everybody was going to gassed, because they needed room for new-- new arrivals.

speaker: interviewer
text: New workers.

speaker: interviewee
text: New workers, so the old ones got to go. And he told me to go left. You go left or right, left or right. Left is dead. Right is still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So instead of going

speaker: interviewer
text: Right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, I went left.

speaker: interviewer
text: You made a mistake.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I made one mistake. I tried to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yea.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they wouldn't let me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew I made the mistake.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, yeah. But what happened this--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my number was taken to the-- to be gassed next day. And then I knew that I'm going to be gassed. I had nothing to lose.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was after curfew already. Nobody was allowed in or out. You just stay in the hoods. I walked out on my own, and I started to walk. And the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] those posts they had, you know, to watch, the watch post? The guard, the guard posts.

speaker: interviewee
text: The guards. But they--they have the guns loaded in case.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just in case. And I walked through, because you have to walk from that locker to the hospital. Because I know Mrs. Felix was in there. And I said, I have to tell her that tomorrow I'm going to be gassed. And I didn't care. I had-- I don't know. I know they're going to shoot me. But I just walked. I'd rather be shot than spend another night. Overnight they keep you there, and then they gas you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they didn't even-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't stop you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't stop, no. And I walked through how many of them. And once, and they didn't stop me. But I don't know why. They didn't stop me. Maybe God stopped them. I don't know. And I walked in and that [INAUDIBLE] and I asked for Mrs. Felix. The-- whoever I met, one of them supposed to be a nurse. And she came out, because she was, you know, why I'm here. Because it-- it's-- it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's curfew.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's curfew. Where-- what am I doing? And I told her. She said, you're not shot, and you walked through? I said, you see, I'm right here. But let me tell you, I was picked in the selection. I made a wrong move. He told me to go right, and I made a mistake. And, you know, this is it. And I'm-- they took my number. And I'm going on the 25th block. [SIGHS] She couldn't believe it. She was just so frustrated. She said, I'll see what I can do. She went to the office to the [NON-ENGLISH]. She said, I have to make sure that your number's there. Because I don't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gutsy lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't believe it. And she went through. And then the secretary, you know, from our block, the woman from Hungary--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yea.

speaker: interviewee
text: That Hungarian. And she came over, she said that your number wasn't even in there, that sort of a-- it wasn't-- it was a mistake.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And even if they wouldn't make that mistake, I couldn't be gassed. Because I have a sentence. I'm [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: You're a political prisoner.

speaker: interviewee
text: A political prisoner. I only there to--

speaker: interviewer
text: To starve to death.

speaker: interviewee
text: To starve to death, to [NON-ENGLISH]. That's it. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saved.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. But the night I went through and everything. And I went back, would you believe it? I walked back?

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked back to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And nothing happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And nothing happened. Maybe they thought, those guards, thought that I'm supposed to be out? Because I was the only one that I have some kind of a function or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: A [NON-ENGLISH], yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so I'm alive again. I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you heard that the Russians were approaching.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I-- we heard that the Russians, and they're going to transfer us, you know, from this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Birkenau to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So, next door actually.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was right next door anyway. And this-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went there. And the-- the conditions were ex-- what a difference. And everything, the blankets were not, you know, with bugs.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words from Birkenau to Auschwitz, Auschwitz was better than Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was no comparison.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Birkenau, was the-- was the death camp actually.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Auschwitz was a holding camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz was a camp too, that-- you know. But in Birkenau, there's no way out.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's no way out, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: No way out. No way out from there, too. But at least you can survive a little longer. The conditions are better, let's put it this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then because-- because of the Russian--

words.text: And then because-- because of the Russian--

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- you are sent a little more water there or to maybe to keep yourself clean or what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because the Russians were coming, they decided to move you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians were coming. The gypsies, they were already taken care of.

speaker: interviewer
text: They killed them already.

speaker: interviewee
text: Out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The gypsies were out. And so for us, I was there until-- then I went with that Czech, a lady. She-- she helped me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later. She is a political prisoner, because she didn't agree with the Nazis. So they put her to, you know, into Birkenau because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was from a high class family, very, very wealthy in Czechoslovakia. And they sent her to Birkenau Auschwitz, because she didn't agree.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then they -- they sent you out.

speaker: interviewee
text: She took me-- she took me in the high commander. And it didn't-- I-- apparently I didn't talk too long. That was already to the end. And I worked in the SS Commander where they cleaned the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Yea.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she had about 20 of them she picked, you know. We were-- we marched up when all the commanders marched out. We were the last ones to march out, only 20 of us. And she used to-- he picked it that one of be the SS high, the highest ranks, you know, that took care. Maybe next to Mengele, he is. He picked her. He came. And he picked her on a roll call. He saw her. She was allowed to wear civilian clothes. And they didn't shaved her hair. She had beautiful, like a chestnut color hair, the most beautiful woman I've ever seen in my life. And she received packages, every week a package from home. So she used to give me a little garlic or some things, you know, a little bit of something. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she took me under her wings, and my-- my feet started, you know, the wound--

speaker: interviewer
text: They got better.

speaker: interviewee
text: --started a little better. And I could walk better. And then I came to the end when we had to leave. There was an announcement that the Russians are coming and everybody's got to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this begin the death march.

speaker: interviewee
text: That began the death march.

speaker: interviewer
text: To-- to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Bergen-Belsen. We walked on the death march.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long?

speaker: interviewee
text: So my feet started to get worse. Because, you know, I stopped this commander. She gave me a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, and that started to heal. And I got worse. And then the only thing she get-- she got me the pair of shoes instead of the wood. I got a pair shoes she got me. And that shoe-- those shoes saved my life. So I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long-- how long did you march between--

speaker: interviewee
text: We marched about two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I half-- a half a night, two days. And the third, we stopped in a movie. It was a movie house. You know, by marching, there's so many people, woman. It was only woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Died. Either, you know, you try to make a wrong move the dog tear you apart, you know, the German shepherds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or, when you behind, a little behind, or you got out of line, they kick you in. And they didn't waste a bullet on you. They just kick you in, in the ditches. And you laid there until you die, or the ones that are already dead. It was just the most horrible thing you can only imagine. And there was already snow, high snow, you know. The cold was so very cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my feet fave up on me. I-- they swell up.  I didn't felt I walked like on stilts, you know, like wooden feet. On stilts, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on stilts. And then we hit this-- we gonna rest for a couple hours. Because the soldiers--

speaker: interviewer
text: They needed rest.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, they need rest. And I think they left for-- for a while. They went, I think, to the village or something to look for food or what. You know that half of them disappeared?

speaker: interviewer
text: They deserted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Half-- no half of the-- of the-- of the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Whoever had-- had shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean they ran. They ran away.

speaker: interviewee
text: They run into the forest. They run into the, you know, they were pine forests. They-- I remember and they ran.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The ones that can still--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had some strength.

speaker: interviewee
text: Strength. I looked around, and then I looked around the movie house. And I was sitting. I took my shoes off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mistake.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the biggest mistake I ever made. My feet were very swollen. And I couldn't put my-- my-- my feet in anymore. That's how swollen  they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you finally squeeze them back into the shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just jiggled in, in. And I walked, you know, my heels were out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The laces, I couldn't lace it.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's hard walking that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And that's how I-- I forced-- I forced-- and then I forced myself walking. I forced. It was just the pins and the pain, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, and then we started to walk. They said, we have to continue. But they didn't give a damn anymore. The-- those-- you know, Werhmacht, whatever they were, they-- the soldiers, they would tired. They were tired. And the dogs, I think, were hungry. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And we didn't felt any hunger. You didn't feel any hunger.

speaker: interviewee
text: We felt that we're going to die any minute of starvation and cold, and I swell up. I really did.

speaker: interviewer
text: You finally got to Bergen-Belsen. What happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, I got to Bergen-Belsen. There was in those cars they put us in from, you know, the-- the cattle cars. That was unbelievable what was going on, one on top of each other with-- with feces, you know. And man and woman, one on top of each other, the screaming, the hunger. And we finally reached Bergen-Belsen. We couldn't believe it was like a paradise. We just grabbed-- I-- I-- grabbed, you know, like a bed, those bunk beds on straw. And I said, this is where I'm going to stay. I couldn't move anyway, couldn't stay there. In the morning, just overnight, we-- we rested. And they took us to a place. It was-- where I winded up is-- It was just a big, big empty shack I would call. It was nothing there, but when I went in a lot of people, you know. And they was sitting, like you sit like the-- the Japanese eat in the sit, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Crouched.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- yeah. In Japanese you sit there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they kept-- and I just find a empty spot. Because you couldn't find a spot. I just squeezed myself in there. And I sat there. And I was hallucinating. I think I didn't know where I am and what's going on. I was-- I wasn't all there. I just felt that's the end. I gave up then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-mm. And the following morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: I give up. The following morning I heard [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Roll call.

speaker: interviewee
text: Roll call. The door she opened, you know, and she recognized me. You know, the one, you know from Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, she recognized. And she tried, everybody out, out. She would scream. Nobody moved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody moved because?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody moved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I couldn't move. I couldn't get out. I couldn't get up. That's it. I just couldn't get up. That's it. She just walked over. And I don't know really what's happened, because nobody moved. And she took-- she grabbed me by the hand, and she pulled me out. She pulled me out, and she took me out. And I was-- she took me in, into a kitchen where they cooked that soup. And she put me down on a chair. And she told the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The cook?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cook. She was also Hungarian. Because they all, you know, helped each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They all had good positions. They were fluent in German and-- you know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she put me down. She said to give me an apron to cover the wounds, my feet, and to give me a-- a bowl of, you know, potatoes to peal.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you looked like you worked there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, like I work. Of course, you have to. Otherwise you--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're finished. So, and she told her to give me and the-- when they-- in those kettles where there-- that soup, that mishmash is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: To take out if there is a little bit of meat or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the bottom, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the bottom-- if, you  know, to bring me once in awhile. And she did. That cook, she did. And I had a-- a while to eat a little bit. And I felt, you know, every day a little better. And the, you know, pus was, you know, it was still bleeding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: My feet were bleeding.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were getting better.

speaker: interviewee
text: Better, because the swelling keeps going down, then I didn't had that pain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I couldn't just sit like this constantly with the potatoes make believe I peel it. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So she said to me, I started-- I knew by myself that I have to help myself, or I'm  dead here. So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I'm going to help like that carrying out those kettles. You know, four woman it takes, you know, to grab those kettles--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, they're heavy.

speaker: interviewee
text: -and carry out, two in the front and two in the back. So-- and one of that I know, the girls that I knew from Birkenau, she said she knew my condition. She know that I'm at the end. She said, you got to-- you got to stay on. You got to hold-- hold on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hold on, please hold on. And she said, hold on she wasn't back to me and make believe that you carry. But I walked actual.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how I hide it. And every day, I felt a little better. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was transferred from data to a block where I met Anne Frank.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was blocked 20?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know who it was. I had no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't you do something? You brought some food back and they sentenced you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yea, tell me about that quickly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Before that, see I--I grabbed one--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --before the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. While I was helping with those kettles--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I-- somebody called out, oh, you the baby. You the baby. You're the youngest. You're the baby. Oh, she thought that I'm in her kitchen that I have access to--

speaker: interviewer
text: To food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Food or something. And she looked a bag of bones. And she told me who she is. I couldn't believe it. They were the most-- the richest people in the city, probably like you would say New York, Rockefeller was the rich, something well-known, the Ehrlich's. I think somebody's still in Israel alive. Because one of them went-- or two of them went to Israel to save themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You'll tell me the rest of the story in a minute, OK? We'll continue.
